preamble: Deaffrication is a common phonetic process where an affricate loses its fricative component, resulting in a plosive. This specific shift involves the voiced alveolo-palatal affricate [d͡ʒ] transforming into a voiced palatal plosive [ɟ]. This phenomenon is observed in certain varieties of Spanish, particularly in contexts of language contact.
phoneticEffects: Deaffrication, loss of frication, simplification of articulation from affricate to plosive

examples.note: Deaffrication of the alveolo-palatal affricate, often a realization of <y> and <ll>, to a palatal plosive. This is part of a broader deaffrication chain [d͡ʒ] > [ɟʝ] > [ɟ] > [ʝ].

sources: Gabriel, C., Pešková, A., & Ewald, T. (2020). How much like Guarani does Paraguayan Spanish sound? The pronunciation of <y> and <ll>. In C. Gabriel, A. Pešková, & M. Selig (Eds.), Contact, variation and change in Romance and beyond. Studies in honor of Trudel Meisenburg (pp. 39-56). Erich Schmidt Verlag.
